 "title": "Colo-MW geography weights are wrong by construction for the 29% enterprise slice — the Netherlands cell is partly phantom",
 "narrative": "Every geo share was apportioned on Statista colocation MW, but the segment axis includes 29% enterprise on-premise demand — and enterprise machine rooms are distributed by industrial base, not by where Amsterdam colo campuses stand. EUDCA's own framing says colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power; the missing third has a different map. NL is the most colo-concentrated market in the set (~70% of its capacity in Amsterdam metro), while Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-base method gives 0.13 vs the curated 0.08) and Germany's installed load exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW. Multiplying colo-weighted geography by an enterprise segment share manufactures enterprise PDU demand in the Netherlands that physically sits in German and Swiss machine rooms. Anyone sizing a DACH enterprise channel off these weights will staff the wrong countries.",

## the EUDCA

It is with great pride as president of the European Data Centre Association (EUDCA), I can present the second edition of our State of European Data Centres, for 2026.

Building on last year’s milestone achievement, it is only possible through the invaluable contribution and cooperation of our members, and we are excited to share the results of this comprehensive research with the public. This year has seen the deepening impact of artificial intelligence, the continued emergence of new centres of activity, and the constant issue of energy availability. Building on the expanding resource that is the Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) data, the report reflects an industry that is experiencing rapid growth even amid the challenges of a more demanding regulatory environment and changing geopolitical and cybersecurity landscapes. # Inaugural Flagship Report - State of European Data Centres 2025 Click here to download​​

## State of European Data Centres 2025

The State of European Data Centres 2025 is the inaugural flagship report from the European Data Centre Association (EUDCA), offering an unprecedented deep dive into the infrastructure that powers Europe’s digital economy. Developed in collaboration with National Trade Associations and leading researchers, this full report goes far beyond the management summary - presenting a large, data-driven examination of the economic, environmental, and technological landscape of Europe’s colocation and hyperscale data centres.

Inside, the report reveals how €100 billion in investments are reshaping Europe’s data centre landscape, advancing AI, boosting digital sovereignty, and expanding from FLAP-D hubs to fast-growing Nordic and Southern regions. It highlights a projected €83.8 billion GDP impact and over 80,000 jobs by 2030, while addressing key challenges like power access, skills shortages, and sustainability, underscored by the sector’s use of 94% renewable energy.

Whether you’re a policymaker, investor, data centre operator, or digital strategist, this report is the critical intelligence you need to navigate the future of digital infrastructure in Europe.

### Executive Summary

The growth of the digital economy and digitalisa tion is accompanied by a strong increase in the need for digital infrastructure and data centres (DCs). Data centres have thus developed into one of the fastest growing, most energy-intensive sectors in Germany. With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Ger many is already the largest centre for digital infra structure in Europe. The central location, political stability, an electricity supply with above-average reliability and one of the largest internet connec tions in the world combine to make Germany a strategically advantageous place for national and international providers alike. ### Romania on the Rise: Featured in the EUDCA 2026 Data Center Report

The European Data Centre Association (EUDCA) recently published the State of European Data Centres 2026 report, showing that Europe is investing heavily in its digital future: between 2026 and 2031, data centers are expected to attract €176 billion. While the FLAP-D region dominates the scene with over 6,600 MW of IT power in 2026, Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) is preparing to catch up, totaling 883 MW. Romania is mentioned three times in the report, ranking among the fastest-growing colocation markets in the region, confirming that the local digital infrastructure is entering a phase of expansion.

## EUDCA 2026 Highlights Romania and CEE Growth

In Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), Switzerland currently holds the largest IT capacity in the colocation sector (302 MW in 2026, 464 MW in 2031), but Poland could surpass it, with estimated growth from 197 MW in 2024 to 511 MW in 2031 (CAGR 15%), according to EUDCA. Poland maintains an annual growth of 15% in the hyperscale sector as well.

Alongside Poland, Romania is among the markets with the highest annual growth (14%), with IT capacity projected to increase from 27 MW in 2025 to 66 MW in 2031. Both countries are only surpassed by Croatia (30% annual growth) in the region.

# Colocation data center supply in Europe 2019-2024
Published by[Statista Research Department](https://www.statista.com/aboutus/our-research-commitment),Jan 30, 2025
[](#statisticContainer)
Colocation data center supply in Europe's four largest markets (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, and Paris) has increased year-on-year since 2019. In the first quarter of 2023, the total colocation data center supply was 4,243 megawatts. Colocation refers to data centers which rent out equipment, space and bandwidth to retail customers.

## Netherlands Real Estate Market Outlook 2025

By Frank Verwoerd Erik Langens

By Frank Verwoerd Erik Langens

Data centres

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Worldwide, the data centres market has grown significantly in recent years. This is partly due to cloud computing, big data, the (further) increasing digitalisation and the increasing demand for data services such as AI. Together with Frankfurt, London, Paris and Dublin, Amsterdam is one of the five most important colocation markets[14] in the European data centre market. A position owed to a strategic location and a good digital infrastructure. But while demand for data services and capacity in our country continues to increase, growth in the Netherlands is limited. This results in scarcity.

### Trends and developments

- The amount of data is growing exponentially due to developments such as AI and digitization. As a result, the demand for data centre is also increasing. In five years' time, this has led to a significant decrease in vacancy in and around Amsterdam: from 24% to almost 9%. This is particularly low in the data centre sector. Moreover, due to power grid congestion and restrictive regulations, there is hardly any room for expansion. The vacancy rate is therefore only expected to decrease further.

## New State of European Data Centres 2026 report

## State of European Data Centres 2026

The State of European Data Centres 2026 is EUDCA’s flagship annual report, delivering a data-driven overview of Europe’s data centre sector. Developed with national trade associations (NTAs) and leading researchers, the report examines the infrastructure supporting Europe’s digital economy and the forces that will shape its future.

The 2026 edition shows that commercial colocation and hyperscale facilities now provide more than two-thirds of Europe’s IT power, driven by the growing demand for cloud and AI. It explores shifting regional growth patterns across Southern Europe and the Nordics, alongside the impact of new connectivity, renewable energy investment and rising power constraints.

With European IT power demand forecast to grow at a CAGR of 17% through 2031, the report analyses the key challenges, including access to power, cooling innovation, and its growing socio-economic and sustainability contribution. Colocation data centres significantly advanced Europe's 2030 energy, water, and climate goals in 2025 alone, contributing €53 billion to the continent's GDP.

Whether you’re a policymaker, investor, data centre operator, or digital strategist, this report is the critical intelligence you need to navigate the future of digital infrastructure in Europe.

# EUDCA publishes its new 2026 report

#### Author: Joe Peck

The European Data Centre Association(EUDCA), the representative body of the European data centre community, has announced the publication of its 2026 State of European Data Centres report.

Building upon regional benchmarks established in last year’s report, the new data reveals a European market that has moved beyond the era of hub-centric development and is evolving into a distributed, energy-integrated, and AI-driven digital ecosystem.

Europe’s data centre sector is shown to be entering a period of exceptional expansion, structural diversification, and rapid technological transformation, driven by AI hyper-expansion. However, its ability to fully exploit potential growth is threatened by energy availability and access.

The new EUDCA report finds European market growth not only within traditional centres – such as the Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin (FLAP-D) – but also rapidly decentralising across Southern Europe, the Nordics, Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), and selected Tier2 metros.

Moving from cloud-led growth to AI demand, data centres are now recognised as critical infrastructure underpinning Europe’s competitiveness and security.

## Growth and investment in Europe’s data centres

provide more than two-thirds of Europe’s IT power, driven by the growing demand for cloud and AI."},{"url":"https://www.cloudfest.com/blog/frankfurt-overtake-london-colocation-data-center-market-educa-report","stance":"supports","quote":"With over two-thirds of “IT power” delivered by landscape."},{"url":"https://www.bundeswirtschaftsministerium.de/Redaktion/EN/Publikationen/Digitale-Welt/status-and-development-of-the-german-data-centre-landscape-executive-summary.pdf?__blob=publicationFile&v=2","stance":"supports","quote":"With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Ger many is already the largest centre for digital infra structure in Europe."},{"url":"https://www.germandatacenters.com/fileadmin/images/DCIRD-24/Data-Center-Impact-Report-Deutschland-2024_EN.pdf","stance":"supports","quote":"1,955 MW IT power in Germany approx.  ... thereof 69 % in colocation data centers"},{"url":"https://www.cbre.com/insights/books/nl-real-estate-market-outlook-2025/data-centres","stance":"silent","quote":"With a capacity of more than 500 megawatts, Amsterdam is currently the third largest colocation data centre hub in Europe."},{"url":"https://www.statista.com/statistics/1659712/europe-colocation-data-center-power-by-country/","stance":"silent","quote":"2025 | 1,772 | 1,737 | 951 | 813 | 543 | 451 | 363 | 274 | 286 | 228 | 226"}],"status":"corroborated","statusRationale":"The load-bearing trigger premise — that commercial colocation + hyperscale supply only ~two-thirds of Europe's IT power, leaving a differently-distributed ~one-third of enterprise/on-prem load invisible to the colo-MW denominator — is directly and independently confirmed by EUDCA's own 2026 framing and by CloudFest's report coverage. The German figures corroborate the divergence direction: BMWK reports >2,700 MW installed IT power vs the ~1,458–1,737 MW colo figure in Statista, a gap consistent with the claimed ~1,000MW installed-load excess, and German Datacenters shows only 69% of DC capacity is colocation. This supports the mechanism that multiplying a colo-derived geo vector by a 29% enterprise segment share misallocates enterprise demand. The NL-specific magnitude (that its installed-load share differs materially from its ~0.28 colo share) is not directly quantified in the results, so the exact phantom size remains partly unconfirmed, but the core mechanism premise is materially supported.","evidence":[{"title":"New 2026 State of European Data Centres","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"EUDCA","date":"2026","excerpt":"The 2026 edition shows that commercial colocation and hyperscale facilities now provide more than two-thirds of Europe’s IT power, driven by the growing demand for cloud and AI.","url":"https://www.eudca.org/new-2026-state-of-european-data-centres"},{"title":"Status and development of the German data centre landscape – Executive Summary","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"BMWK (German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs)","date":"2025-03","excerpt":"With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Germany is already the largest centre for digital infrastructure in Europe.","url":"https://www.bundeswirtschaftsministerium.de/Redaktion/EN/Publikationen/Digitale-Welt/status-and-development-of-the-german-data-centre-landscape-executive-summary.pdf?__blob=publicationFile&v=2"},{"title":"Data Center Impact Report Deutschland","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"German Datacenters Association","date":"2024","excerpt":"1,955 MW IT power in Germany approx. ... thereof 69 % in colocation data centers","url":"https://www.germandatacenters.com/fileadmin/images/DCIRD-24/Data-Center-Impact-Report-Deutschland-2024_EN.pdf"}],"revisedLikelihood":0.6,"revisionRationale":"The trigger premise (colo+hyperscale ≈ two-thirds of IT power, missing third differently distributed) is now directly confirmed by EUDCA's own framing rather than merely inferred, and German installed-vs-colo divergence (>2,700MW installed vs ~1,700MW colo; only 69% colo share) is corroborated by two independent official sources in the predicted direction. This raises confidence in the mechanism. The NL phantom magnitude specifically remains unquantified in the evidence, so the increase is bounded modestly above the prior 0.5."}
